title: On Dimension and Absolute Continuity of Self-Similar Measures
speaker: Constantin Kogler
abstract:
A similarity is a map from Rd to itself that uniformly scales distances. When one repeatedly applies randomly chosen contracting similarities, the resulting Markov chain converges to a limiting stationary distribution known as a self-similar measure. The central problems in the study of self-similar measures are to determine their dimension and to understand when they are absolutely continuous, meaning that they admit a density with respect to the Lebesgue measure. 
I will present joint work with Samuel Kittle addressing these questions. Indeed, we prove Hochman’s result on the dimension of self-similar measures under a weakened Diophantine assumption. Moreover, we establish numerous novel explicit examples of absolutely continuous self-similar measures. In fact, we give the first inhomogeneous examples in dimension 1 and 2 and construct examples for essentially any given rotations and translations, provided they have algebraic coefficients. Moreover, we strengthen Varju’s result for Bernoulli convolutions and Lindenstrauss-Varju’s result in dimension greater than or equal to 3
tags: Joint IAS/PU Groups and Dynamics Seminar

proof_stages.label: Contractive similarities and stationary self-similar measures
proof_stages.summary: The talk defines similarities as rotation-scaling-translation maps, fixes a finitely supported probability measure on contractive similarities, and identifies its unique stationary measure as the self-similar measure obtained from the associated random walk.

proof_stages.label: Dimension and absolute-continuity questions
proof_stages.summary: The two main problems are posed: compute the dimension of the self-similar measure and determine when it is absolutely continuous with respect to Lebesgue measure.

proof_stages.label: Entropy-Lyapunov dimension conjecture
proof_stages.summary: Lyapunov exponent and random-walk entropy are introduced to give the general upper bound dim ν≤min(h/χ,d), and after irreducibility and non-degeneracy assumptions this becomes the generalized exact-overlaps conjectural formula.

proof_stages.label: Separation conditions for the dimension formula
proof_stages.summary: A separation scale m_n is defined, algebraic and Hochman-type separation hypotheses are recalled, and the speaker's new result is stated: a weaker polylogarithmic separation bound at all scales suffices for the dimension formula, with consequences via Varjú's work for Bernoulli-type parameters.

proof_stages.label: Uniform absolute-continuity theorem
proof_stages.summary: The absolute-continuity conjecture h/χ>d is formulated, a quantitative separation exponent S_μ and irreducible rotation set are introduced, and a theorem is stated giving absolute continuity when probabilities are bounded below, rotations stay in a compact irreducible region, and the entropy/Lyapunov ratio is sufficiently large relative to S_μ.

proof_stages.label: Arithmetic and rotation-group corollaries
proof_stages.summary: The theorem is made concrete by bounding S_μ through algebraic height, deriving Bernoulli and broad algebraic-family corollaries, and explaining high-dimensional entropy lower bounds from non-solvable rotation groups and their relation to Lindenstrauss-Varjú spectral-gap results.

proof_stages.label: Variance-summation proof method
proof_stages.summary: The proof is sketched by decomposing finite random-walk images of the stationary measure into conditionally independent summands, using separation to obtain variance bounds at many scales, and summing those scale-wise variance contributions to reach the dimension and absolute-continuity conclusions.

examples.name: Real Bernoulli convolutions
examples.note: The one-dimensional two-map system x↦λx±1 is used as the simplest self-similar measure and later as a benchmark for dimension and absolute-continuity results.
examples.engagement_reason: he explicitly instantiates the self-similar construction with the two maps x↦λx±1 and later specializes absolute-continuity bounds to rational Bernoulli parameters

examples.name: Common fixed point Dirac obstruction
examples.note: If all similarities have the same fixed point, the stationary measure is just a Dirac mass, showing why a non-degeneracy assumption is needed.
examples.engagement_reason: he only states the common-fixed-point case gives a Dirac stationary measure as an obstruction, without working through a concrete instance

examples.name: Algebraic rotations with contractions q/(q+i)
examples.note: A broad explicit algebraic family in any dimension illustrates that the absolute-continuity theorem yields concrete new self-similar measures, including variable-contraction examples in dimension one.
examples.engagement_reason: he constructs the family with fixed algebraic rotations/translations and contractions q/(q+i), then applies the theorem to conclude absolute continuity for large prime q
